search.query: index="email" (sourcetype="dmarc:failure" OR sourcetype="dmarc:forensic") (parsed_sample.headers.From=$header_from$ OR NOT parsed_sample.headers.From=*) (parsed_sample.headers.To=$header_to$ OR NOT parsed_sample.headers.To=*) (parsed_sample.headers.Subject=$header_subject$ OR NOT parsed_sample.headers.Subject=*) (source.ip_address=$source_ip_address$ OR NOT source.ip_address=*) (source.reverse_dns=$source_reverse_dns$ OR NOT source.reverse_dns=*) (source.country=$source_country$ OR NOT source.country=*) | table *

html.p: DMARC failure reports (RUF) contain an email sample that failed DMARC. These can be very useful for DMARC troubleshooting and phishing investigations. However, most email providers do not send failure reports, or may only supply the message headers for privacy reasons.
If you want to ensure that email samples are not saved here, do not set a ruf address in your domain's DMARC record.

search.query: | rename parsed_sample.from.address as from, parsed_sample.subject as subject, parsed_sample.reply_to{}.address as reply_to | table arrival_date_utc, source.ip_address, "from", subject, reply_to, authentication_results | sort -arrival_date_utc
